text.content:
You know, sometimes the oldest fortification techniques were still the best. Whirlwind sang a s she pushed the tumbler through the packed earth. "Diggin a ditch, gotta dig that ditch. Soon we'll have a ditch, cuz you can't stop this bit—"

"Please stop, Whirlwind." I tapped the screen of the hand-held scanner. It was one of those "fancy" ones without the handle. People must've had money to burn if they thought "sleek" was more useful than... usefulness. The screen showed a wire-mesh of the terrain and the pings where the M-bats were set up.

Whirlwind, still standing in the ditch, crossed her arms atop the dirt pile. "Ditch is done," she grinned.

"Awesome. Mind doing one more for me?" Still staring at the awful scanner, I turned east - no west… weast .

"Sure," she panted, climbing out of the hole, and followed me to the next M-bat. Except...

I looked up from the scanner, and turned. "Uhh…" I looked down at the pinging again. "We should be right on top of it."

"You're reading it wrong," Whirlwind assumed. I turned to show her. And that's when I felt my heel compress the landmine.

Combat Action [90] Take cover or bolster defenses

Creature Behavior [51] Lurer

Envision the Starship [44] Navigation logs contain coordinates to locations that do not - or should not - exist

"Go, go, go!" Morgan shouted, outrunning the fissures splitting open in the ground. The starhop couldn't have been more than fifty meters ahead. They all booked it, James leading the way because he was the only one who could consistently keep his bearings in these slime caverns. There was no mistaking the sound of the massive, toothed legs of the whale-splitters tearing open the ground, gashing the gunk all the way to the rock beneath. Jump didn't have to look back to know the behemoths were on their tail, but she did look back, cross-laser drawn. A flash of lavender light alit the cavern walls, blindingly reflecting off the goo. The hash of the beams carved into one of the splitter's hides! It fell back in pain and Jump aimed at another. Suddenly the slick ground caught her boot and her foot shot out from beneath her. Jump slammed back, and goo enveloped her, sinking into her nose and mouth. Near instantly she felt a hand grab her bicep and Morgan dragged her back onto her feet. Jump hacked and gulped, then hacked and gasped as they fled for the starhop while James laid down cover fire. They were nearly there. In th panic, Morgan didn't see the brighter green globs of the slime slither into Jump's nose, mouth, ears, and eyes. None of the humans could see—or feel—the upsilon radiation begin to streak out o f Jump's body. But the splitters could. Something connected to itself within Jump's body, exerting itself to near death until the splitters backed off.

Starship History [85] Taken while fleeing an attack or disaster

Revealed Creature Aspect [72] Parasitic

Encountered Creature Behavior [77] Protector

Tidal forces: it's just gravity. But gravity doesn't only affect oceans.

Alaba stood beneath the shadow of the gas giant, Olorun, on the moon of Obatala. The moon orbited far enough from Olorun that the planet didn't shred it, but close enough that the unfathomable force of gravity stretched and warped the rock as it spun in space, liquifying the mantle and crust to create an ever-erupting furnace world.

Alaba, encased in her thermairon suit, gazed into the mouth of the cave where flows of dim lava lit the basalt crystals. "Please," she called within. "If you show me how, I swear upon the iron in my blood, I will stop The Collision."

The lava gently pushed over itself as it flowed...

"Please. Are you there?"

...Within the depths, a dim blue light began to shine and grow until it overpowered the lava's red. From the darkness, a cobalt statue of a hero long passed, appeared before Alaba.

Character Goal [25] Discover a truth

Planetary Class [24] Furnace World

Starship Initial Contact [66] Asking for help

The 8-bit text and tiny screen used so little power it flickered. Karina leaned against the side of the cockpit, sipping at caffeinated milk. She stretched her back, then finally pushed the chair the full six inches back and unsnapped the mid controls so she could actually stretch her legs. Sometimes, your contribution was just waiting. Isolation was beginning to feel like emotional claustrophobia. And this might be why they'd lose the war. It's hard to win against those tactics. But go low tech enough, rely enough of pure will of human ingenuity, and you've got a fighting chance. Karina wrenched her neck to look back through the four-by-six-foot cargo hold to see the black and green striped loader bot just outside it. She already thought they were cute, but whoever owned it had put googly eyes on the suction lifter and a bowtie on the front of the battery. Uh-dorable. Karina felt the suspension bounce as the second, then the third crate got loaded up, and the entire two-person-max ship rocked as the cargo door slammed shut. The red, sensor light turned off to show it had sealed airtight. Karina put her drink back into its ring, adjusted her chair for flight again, and snapped the mid levers back by her side. She watched the flickering screen, waiting. Finally... "Go." Karina lifted vertically, and gunned it. The exabytes of memory in her hold was only part of t h e answer. But the hunter-bots couldn't scrub it from transfer if it was never uploaded to the web. Apparently at one time they called this "snail mail."

Action [55] Coordinate

Theme [43] Opportunity

Character Goal [84] Gain knowledge

Only red light could filter through the thick atmosphere, and so the trees' natural color appeared glossy black. The wide sweep of wings of the hawkons as they soared down from the cliffs appeared black. The loamy soil, rich and fertile, appeared black. The only other natural light here on this heavily gaseous moon was from the dull red lava which flowed from fissures and nurtured the land. Red lava: the same wavelength of light that made it through the heavy skies.

The trees and birds and slowly flowing soil overcame the prefab houses and unemptied crates. Humans could not bear the darkness and rusty clouds. Their skin wanted light, their eyes wanted light, their minds wanted light.

But, of course, they were surrounded by light. The creatures that evolved here saw every brilliant, minute difference in tiny portions of infrared wavelengths. To them red was sometimes yellow, red was sometimes green, and red was sometimes blue. Their world shone as vibrant as any other because it was theirs.

But the humans could not bear it. So, they laid down.

Or they died. Or they left.

Settlement Trouble [22] Deprived of a resource

Theme [81] Spirit

Creature First Look [84] Prominent wings or fins

Eppi was already drunk. Zhuzh gulped at the booze, "Alf shitting, that's bad! What's the ape?"

Eppi grinned. "Filifty-seven-point-eight!"

Zhuzh looked down at her mug in awe and murmured, "Alf shitting, that's gooood."

Mister laughed, "From now on, every mission is a jungle mission!" The team had grabbed so much fruit that the flash freezer was full, so they'd stuffed the rest into the auto-still and hours later this was the horrifically wonderful result.

Eppi slammed the rest of the newborn brandy and chanted, "Black. Hole. Boots! Black. Hole. Boots!" And so, they all put on their magnetic boots and tried, failed, and flailed to walk around in the artificial gravity, drunk off their assess. Eppi was the first to make it all the way from the sludge pump to the piolet's seat, with raucous cheers all around. She threw her arms out in victory, booze sloshing from her mug and across the ship's console. All three of them swore viciously, unsnapping the magnetism and rushing over. "What'd I do? How bad izzit?" Eppi urged as Mister checked over the flashing lights. Only one had gone out.

"Yep," he said. "The nebulizer finally took its last breath."

Zhuzh snort-laughed, then apologized.

Mister shrugged. "We can just detour to Yeller Five. Get a new one."

Neither one saw Eppi waggle her eyebrows. It was just too easy to get them to do whatever she wanted .

Story Complication [6] Crucial equipment or device is sabotaged.

First Look [4] Accompanied.

Character Goal [44] Obtain an object.

Numbers make up the universe. The collision of particles are mathematically predicted, every single one, already set upon its course, bringing forth a predetermined future. But it can never be predicted, because once you observe the particle, you have changed its course.

And so, the Collective of the Radiant Specters predicted all of them. A database that contains every permutation, capable of searching out each and every option, in every language, real, undiscovered, or imagined. A true tower of babble. It glows with the super computer's dim heat. Every book ever written, and every book that hasn't yet been written, could be found here. What's your favorite Shakespeare play? Oh? But which version? They are infinite. Every book of science, of math, of undiscovered physics that could change civilization. And every autobiography. Every possible version of that life's autobiography. As more and more is known, more possibilities are permutated.

Gathered behind firewall after firewall, it's all protected here for eternity.

Gathered behind firewall after firewall, it's all protected here.

Gathered behind firewall, it's all protected here.

Gathered behind firewall.

Gathered.

Identity [73] Specters

The starship strained to reel in the massive harness. The gears clicked and the tribrid engines shifted back and forth. "You've got this, Scuttlebooty," I cooed at the starship, my little home and companion. I looked back at the behemoth that the harness attempted to lift. Massive a s a n orca whale, an d furry enough to get lost in, this beastie never even opened her beach ball-sized eyes as the harness slowly lifted her out of the hibernation hole. Careful of her barbs, I patted her ankle as she was lifted up into Scuttlebooty's booty-bay. "Let's get you home, tyranna-gopher-rex." I slipped my foot into the stirrup connected to the cable dangling out of Scuttlebooty's cargo hold, I held on and got reeled in myself.

The bay door sealed shut and I made my way to the piolet seat. My bald head itched, but I didn't take m y spacesuit's helmet off. Just because these gopher-a-sauruses needed a decent amount of radiation to stay happy and warm on their way back to their homeworld, didn't mean I needed any more radiation than necessary. My mRNA unraveling might be stage 8, but I didn't need to die any faster. Besides—| looked back at the snoozing beastie-the longer I live, the longer I get to do something no healthy person can safely do.

Moments Forged - 8 Flash Fictions/Adventure Starters

From the Moments Forged website

Moments Forged is a set of 8 flash fiction stories, all created with random Oracle prompts from Ironsworn: Starforged by Shawn Tomkin. After a few casts of the dice I interpreted those Oracles into a quick tale, a moment of time in the vast Forge. Each one is more than a curious, stand-alone story; they are open-ended inspiration to incorporate into your own Starforged game, or to begin your own adventure out in space.
